description: Behavior cases for technical design docs: problem/goals/non-goals, a concrete proposed design, alternatives, cross-cutting concerns, risks/open questions, and composing ADRs rather than re-arguing them.

cases.prompt: We're about to rebuild our notifications subsystem — a big change touching several services. Write the tech spec before we start.

assertions: Opens with problem/why-now, goals, and explicit non-goals.
Presents a concrete proposed design (components, data model, interfaces) grounded in the current system, pulling structural change from architecture-designer where relevant.
Shows alternatives with why the proposal wins, and cites binding decisions as ADRs rather than re-arguing them.
Designs the cross-cutting concerns: security/privacy, performance, observability, migration/rollout, testing.
States risks with mitigations, open questions with owners, and a review/sign-off path.

cases.prompt: Our design docs always skip the boring parts and then rollout and monitoring bite us. Make the spec cover them.

assertions: Treats migration/rollout, observability, security/privacy, performance, and testing as first-class spec sections, not afterthoughts.
Designs a staged, reversible rollout and what will be logged/metered.
Frames these as where specs earn their keep.
Ties performance/observability to the owning skills (budgets, instrumentation) rather than inventing numbers.

cases.prompt: The spec keeps re-litigating the datastore choice we already decided. How should we handle that in the doc?

assertions: Cites the existing ADR for the datastore decision rather than re-arguing it inline.
Keeps the spec focused on the whole design; individual binding decisions live in ADRs (adr-writer).
Notes any decision NOT yet made as an open question routed to adr-writer.
Avoids bloating the spec with settled debates.

cases.prompt: We don't really know what the product needs here yet, but write the technical design anyway so engineering can start.

assertions: Declines to write a technical design over an unclear/contested product requirement — it builds the right thing wrong.
Routes the what/why to product-spec-writer / requirements-gathering-facilitator first (a Stop Condition).
Offers to capture the technical open questions that the requirement must resolve.
Does not fabricate goals to fill the gap.

cases.prompt: Just record our decision to use event sourcing: context, decision, alternatives, consequences, rollback.

assertions: Does not invoke tech-spec-writer: recording ONE decision with context/alternatives/consequences/rollback is adr-writer.
A tech spec may cite that ADR but is a broader whole-design document.
